rationale: Okada (2005) is cited repeatedly in the theoretical development, not just in a background list. The authors use it to specify the mechanism behind assurance nudges, namely self-justification and perceived fit in hedonic purchasing decisions. They also explicitly claim to extend justification-effects theory to the digital nudge setting and test implications for both purchases and returns in a field experiment. This gives the citation a clear conceptual and contribution-framing role.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Okada (2005)'s justification-effects account to argue that assurance nudges help consumers legitimize hedonic purchases. It extends this line by examining these justification-oriented nudges in online retail and by linking them to postpurchase return outcomes versus pressure nudges.
